era_context: Late Archaic period in Greece; the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadnezzar II dominates Mesopotamia and the Levant. In China, the Spring and Autumn period continues with competing states (Lu, Qi, Jin, Chu, etc.). In India, the Vedic period transitions toward early Upanishadic philosophy and the rise of heterodox movements. Egypt remains under Late Period dynasties. The Iron Age is fully established across the Mediterranean, Near East, and much of Europe.

events.title: Thales of Miletus and early Greek natural philosophy
events.description: Thales of Miletus (c. 624–546 BCE), one of the earliest Greek philosophers, is traditionally active during this period. He is credited with founding natural philosophy (physika) and attempting to explain natural phenomena through material causes rather than mythological narratives. Thales is said to have predicted a solar eclipse (traditionally dated to 585 BCE, but the exact date is disputed), studied geometry, and proposed that water is the fundamental substance of all matter. His work marks a significant shift in Greek intellectual culture toward rational inquiry.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: Thales' life dates and specific achievements are known only through later sources written 200+ years after his death. No contemporary writings by Thales survive. The traditional dating of his eclipse prediction to 585 BCE is itself disputed by modern scholars. His activity around 600 BCE is plausible but not confirmed by contemporary evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader Archaic Greek intellectual development

events.title: Nebuchadnezzar II's Levantine campaigns and Tyre siege (ongoing)
events.description: Nebuchadnezzar II's military campaigns in the Levant continue. The siege of Tyre remains ongoing, now in its fifth or sixth year (605–592 BCE). Babylonian administrative records document the continuation of military operations. The campaign consolidates Babylonian hegemony over the region and marks the decline of Phoenician independence.
key_figures: Ethbaal III (King of Tyre, traditional dating)
events.certainty_note: The Babylonian Chronicle documents the general campaign period. Specific events for 600 BCE are not individually detailed in surviving records, but the campaign's continuation is well-established.

events.title: Spring and Autumn period in China: State of Chu expansion
events.description: During the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BCE), the state of Chu in the Yangtze River valley expands its power and influence. Around 600 BCE, Chu is one of the major powers competing for hegemony in China, along with Jin, Qi, and Lu. The period is characterized by frequent warfare, diplomatic alliances, and the gradual consolidation of power among larger states at the expense of smaller ones. Chu's expansion southward and eastward during this period is documented in the Spring and Autumn Annals (Chunqiu).
key_figures: King Zhuang of Chu (r. 613–591 BCE, traditional dating)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Zuo Zhuan (Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals)

events.certainty_note: The Spring and Autumn Annals is a near-contemporary chronicle, though it was compiled and edited over time. The Zuo Zhuan and Shiji are later compilations. The dating of specific events in this period is uncertain; Chinese chronology before 841 BCE is particularly problematic, though 600 BCE falls within the better-attested Spring and Autumn period.
cross_references: Part of broader Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BCE)

events.title: Early Upanishadic philosophy in India
events.description: Around 600 BCE, the early Upanishads are being composed in India, marking a transition from Vedic ritualism toward philosophical inquiry into the nature of reality, the self (atman), and ultimate reality (Brahman). The Upanishads represent a significant intellectual development in Indian thought, moving away from the polytheistic ritualism of the Vedas toward more abstract philosophical and spiritual concepts. This period also sees the emergence of heterodox movements that will eventually lead to Buddhism and Jainism.

sources.name: Upanishads (Isha, Kena, Katha, Mundaka, Mandukya, Aitareya, Taittiriya, Chandogya, Brihadaranyaka)

events.certainty_note: The dating of the Upanishads is highly uncertain. Scholars debate whether they were composed between 1500–500 BCE or more narrowly between 800–500 BCE. The assignment to 600 BCE is conventional and represents the middle of the probable range. No contemporary non-textual evidence (inscriptions, archaeological artifacts) confirms the dating.
cross_references: Precursor to emergence of Buddhism and Jainism (-500 onwards)

disconfirming_evidence: Thales of Miletus is traditionally dated to c. 624–546 BCE, placing him as active around 600 BCE. However, modern scholars note that all biographical information about Thales comes from sources written 200+ years after his death (Aristotle, Herodotus, Diogenes Laërtius). The famous eclipse prediction is traditionally dated to 585 BCE, but this date is disputed; some scholars argue it may have occurred in 603 BCE or not at all. No writings by Thales survive, and it is unclear whether he wrote anything. The dating of the Upanishads is highly contested; some scholars place the earliest Upanishads as late as 500 BCE or even later, while others argue for dates as early as 1500 BCE. The assignment to 600 BCE is a scholarly convention with significant uncertainty. The Spring and Autumn Annals is a near-contemporary chronicle, but it was compiled and edited over centuries, and the dating of specific events is uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 600 BCE is moderate in quality but heavily skewed toward certain regions and types of events. The Babylonian Chronicle provides near-contemporary documentation of Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns. Greek sources are entirely absent for this year; all information about Thales comes from later compilations. Chinese sources (Spring and Autumn Annals) are near-contemporary chronicles, though they were edited over time. Indian sources (Upanishads) are religious texts whose dating is highly uncertain. Egyptian sources are sparse. Sub-Saharan Africa, Mesoamerica, Northern Europe, and most of the world outside the Mediterranean, Near East, China, and India are essentially undocumented. The bias toward elite political and military events is pronounced; economic, social, and cultural developments are largely invisible except where they are embedded in philosophical or religious texts. The survival of sources is heavily dependent on the durability of the medium (clay tablets for Babylonian records, papyrus for Egyptian records, palm leaves and later paper for Indian texts, bamboo strips for Chinese texts).

graph_edges.note: Thales' eclipse prediction (traditional dating 585 BCE) is part of his broader philosophical work
Continuation of Nebuchadnezzar's Levantine campaigns and Tyre siege
Chu's expansion during Spring and Autumn period leads to Battle of Bi in 589 BCE
Early Upanishadic philosophy contributes to emergence of Buddhism and Jainism
